misconceptionSummary: Students confuse multiples with factors, listing numbers that divide into the target rather than the target's times-table members.

visuals.ruleText: Multiples of n are the results of multiplying n by 1, 2, 3, 4, …
visuals.subText: They are the members of n's times table

steps.narration: Multiples of a number are what you get by multiplying it by any positive whole number. The multiples of six are six, twelve, eighteen, twenty-four — the six times table continuing indefinitely.

lines.text: All are greater than or equal to 6

steps.narration: List multiples by multiplying: six, twelve, eighteen, twenty-four, thirty, and so on. Multiples are always equal to or greater than the original number, and there are infinitely many of them.

To test whether a number is a multiple, divide it by the original. If the result is a whole number, it is a multiple. Forty-two divided by six is seven exactly — so forty-two is a multiple. Forty-four does not divide exactly, so it is not.

visuals.label: Multiples = n × 1, n × 2, n × 3, … — the times table of n

steps.narration: Six, twelve, eighteen, and so on. Every multiple of six is divisible by six with no remainder — that is the test for membership.

misconceptionStrip.text: Common mistake: listing factors of 6 (1, 2, 3, 6) when asked for multiples. Multiples of 6 are 6 × 1, 6 × 2, 6 × 3, … — they are always ≥ 6 and continue forever.
misconceptionStrip.audioNarration: Multiples grow — they are the times table. Factors shrink — they are what divides in. Multiples of 6 start at 6 and go up: 6, 12, 18, 24 and so on.
